output.answer:
## Waste Management Rate Increase: Status and Resident Impact

### Is it finalized?

Yes, the memos indicate the rate increase is finalized and already in effect. According to MAR-014, new rates took effect **April 1, 2026**, following two distinct actions:

1. **A contractual trigger**: Under Section 6.2 of the franchise agreements, Waste Management's Return on Revenue falling below 10% on a three-year rolling average automatically triggers rate adjustments (MAR-014).
2. **A Council vote**: On February 11, 2026, the City Council voted to raise the franchise fee from 8% to 14% (MAR-014, confirmed also in FEB-016).

The rate calculation layered both changes together: the old franchise fee was stripped out, a 5.27% CPI increase was applied to the base rate, and then the new 14% franchise fee was added back in (MAR-014).

---

### What does it mean for residents?

The memos suggest residents will see **meaningful bill increases**, with the size depending on service type:

| Service Type | Old Rate | New Rate | Increase |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard 35-gal solid waste / 64-gal recycle | $20.56 | $23.15 | +$2.59 (+12.6%) |
| Senior rate (same service) | $18.38 | $20.70 | +$2.32 (+12.6%) |
| Low Income/Senior rate | $13.79 | $17.36 | +$3.57 (+25.9%) |
| Animal-resistant 96-gal automated | $30.64 | $34.51 | +$3.87 (+12.6%) |
| Cart replacement | $126.27 | $142.20 | +$15.93 (+12.6%) |

*(All figures from MAR-014)*

A few things worth flagging:

- **Low-income and senior residents face the steepest percentage increase** — roughly 26% — compared to about 12-13% for standard and senior rates (MAR-014). The memos do not explain why the low-income/senior rate rises proportionally more than others.
- **The franchise fee hike is the larger driver** for most residents. The CPI component alone would have produced a ~5.27% increase; the jump from 8% to 14% in the franchise fee accounts for the additional burden (MAR-014).
- **There are also behind-the-scenes cost increases** that don't directly hit residents' bills but affect the overall system: city allowances for collection, transfer stations, and the Lockwood Landfill all rose by the same 5.27% CPI factor, and the host fee Waste Management pays the City rose from $0.63 to $0.66 per ton (MAR-013).

---

### Broader context worth noting

The memos suggest this rate increase is happening against a backdrop of **declining consumer confidence** (the Consumer Confidence Index hit its lowest point since May 2014 in January 2026, per MAR-001) and **rising apartment rents** (up 6.6% year-over-year, per MAR-001). The memos do not address whether the Council weighed these economic pressures when voting to raise the franchise fee.

Additionally, FEB-016 notes that a comprehensive periodic review of the Waste Management contract is underway (launched June 2025, running through June 2027), and the City has not conducted a formal audit of Waste Management since before 2018. The memos suggest these oversight mechanisms are incomplete even as rates rise.

MAR-014.title: 2026 Waste Management Rate Increase Notice
MAR-014.tldr: Waste Management notified the City of Reno on March 6, 2026 of rate adjustments effective April 1, 2026, driven by a 5.27% CPI increase for garbage and trash collection from December 2024 to December 2025. Rates are also affected by the City Council's February 11, 2026 vote to raise the franchise fee from 8% to 14%. New rates were calculated by removing the old franchise fee from current rates, applying the CPI increase to the base rate, and then adding the new 14% franchise fee. The memo transmits the formal notification letter and updated rate sheets from Waste Management.
MAR-014.key_stats: CPI increase: 5.27% (December 2024 to December 2025); Franchise fee increase: 8% to 14% (approved February 11, 2026); Rate trigger: Waste Management's Return on Revenue (ROR) falls below 10% on a three-year rolling average; Sample residential rate change: standard 35-gal solid waste / 64-gal recycle cart from $20.56 to $23.15; Senior rate for same service: $18.38 to $20.70; Low Income/Senior rate: $13.79 to $17.36; Animal-resistant 96-gal automated service: $30.64 to $34.51; Cart Replacement: $126.27 to $142.20
MAR-014.key_decisions: City Council voted on February 11, 2026 to increase the franchise fee from 8% to 14%. Rate adjustments are triggered under Section 6.2 of the residential and commercial franchise agreements when Waste Management's ROR falls below 10% on a three-year rolling average.
MAR-014.action_items: Rates take effect April 1, 2026; parties with questions are directed to contact Kendra Kostelecky at Waste Management.

MAR-013.title: 2026 Waste Management Allowance and Host Fees Increase Notice
MAR-013.tldr: Waste Management notified the City of Reno on February 27, 2026 of rate adjustments under the existing disposal agreement, driven by a 5.27% CPI increase for garbage and trash collection from December 2024 to December 2025. City allowances for collection, transfer stations, and Lockwood Landfill will all increase, and the host fee paid by Waste Management to the City rises from $0.63 to $0.66 per ton. Internal solid waste rates for compacted yards, uncompacted yards, and approved recyclables will also increase, though these do not affect rates paid by the public. All changes take effect April 1, 2026.
MAR-013.key_stats: CPI increase: 5.27% (December 2024–December 2025); Collection allowance: $1,287,891.53 → $1,355,763.41 (plus $20,118.32 for clean-up boxes); Transfer Station allowance: $1,249,970.47 → $1,315,843.91; Lockwood Landfill allowance: $60,974.17 → $64,187.51; Host fee: $0.63 → $0.66 per ton; Compacted Yards rate: $17.30 → $18.21; Uncompacted Yards rate: $13.50 → $14.21; Approved Recyclables rate: $65.58 → $69.04 per ton
MAR-013.key_decisions: No council action is explicitly requested; this is an informational notice of contractual rate adjustments triggered automatically by CPI provisions in the disposal agreement.
MAR-013.action_items: Council and City Manager to be aware that new allowance and rate schedules take effect April 1, 2026; Kendra Kostelecky (Waste Management) is available to answer questions.

FEB-016.title: Waste Management Contract and Billing Clarification
FEB-016.tldr: This memo responds to City Council requests from February 11, 2026, clarifying three aspects of the Waste Management franchise agreement: audit procedures, mobile home billing options, and the status of the ongoing periodic review. The City has not exercised its formal audit option since before 2018, though Waste Management is supportive of initiating one. Mobile home communities may choose from commercial, individual residential, or master-billed residential service models. A comprehensive periodic review launched in June 2025 is underway across multiple focus areas, with the review period ending in June 2027.
FEB-016.key_stats: Formal audits limited to 2 during base term (ending November 2029) and 1 during any extension term; last audit exercised prior to 2018. Master-billed residential service billed at 90% occupancy. Composting survey: over 91% of respondents expressed interest in using compost; more than 73% would consider paying for green and food waste pickup. Waste Management ESG targets: 42% reduction in Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions by 2031; 65% beneficial use of captured landfill gas by 2026; 60% increase in material recovery to 25M tons/year by 2030 (with 25% interim milestone by 2025).
FEB-016.key_decisions: Council action on 2/11/26 affected franchise fee levels. An ordinance to repeal Title 5, Sec. 5.90.070 was referred for second reading and potential adoption on February 25, 2026, to remove outdated, duplicated, and contradictory language. Staff awaits direction to initiate a formal third-party audit of Waste Management.
FEB-016.action_items: Staff awaiting Council direction to initiate formal Waste Management audit. Ordinance repealing Title 5, Sec. 5.90.070 scheduled for second reading and potential adoption on February 25, 2026. Staff working with City departments to address underutilization of transfer station allowance. In-depth Reno Direct customer service data analysis in progress. Multiple periodic review focus areas not yet started: Missed Pickups and Resolution Rates, Operational Interviews, and Local Community Survey.

APR-006.title: Roadway Debris and Litter
APR-006.tldr: Unsecured vehicle loads are causing roadway debris and safety hazards along I-80 and routes to the Lockwood Landfill. Nevada statutes prohibit unsecured loads, and 75 citations were issued over the past 24 months, but enforcement alone has not resolved the problem. The City is coordinating with Waste Management and NDOT, launching public communications around free dump days, and pursuing additional funding for a broader education and cleanup program.
APR-006.key_stats: 75 citations issued in the last 24 months under NRS 444.615 and NAC 444.629; free dump days run April 16–25, 2026; cleanup of I-80 areas reported by residents completed March 7, 2026.
APR-006.key_decisions: No formal council votes requested in this memo; memo is informational. Waste Management has committed to tracking unsecured-load instances at Lockwood Landfill. City will post public reminders ahead of free dump days.
APR-006.action_items: 1) Community Engagement & Services to develop a commercial hauler contact list and hold a planning coordination call. 2) City to post load-securing reminders ahead of April 16–25 free dump days. 3) Community Engagement & Services to await outcome of submitted funding application for enforcement tracking, public education, and regional cleanups. 4) Waste Management to begin tracking unsecured-load instances at Lockwood Landfill.

MAR-004.title: January 2026 Clean and Safe Team Homeless Actions and Outcomes
MAR-004.tldr: The Clean and Safe Team's January 2026 report details interdepartmental homeless outreach, enforcement, and maintenance efforts across Reno. Key outcomes include 25 outreach interactions, 6 individuals placed into transitional housing, 73 arrests, 151 citations, and 161 yards of trash collected across 22 cleanup operations. Notable updates include a $53,141 Waste Management donation approved by Council, a new interlocal agreement with NDOT, and the relaunch of the Karma Box Project Work Crew. Reno Direct service requests rose 20% month-over-month, driven largely by a 29% increase in occupied vehicle nuisance requests.
MAR-004.key_stats: 25 outreach interactions; 6 individuals into transitional housing; 3 shelter arrangements; 151 citations (135 RPD, 16 Park/River Rangers); 73 arrests (29 felony, 44 misdemeanor); 99 unsheltered contacts by RPD; 51 occupied vehicles posted; 10 vehicles towed; 3 park exclusions; 37 code enforcement cases; 96 Allied Universal Security site visits; 739 Park and River Ranger site visits; 118 interactions with unsheltered individuals by rangers; 22 cleanups; 161 yards of trash collected; 325 total Reno Direct service requests (20% increase from December's 270); 2 large encampment clean-ups; 17 smaller encampment clean-ups
MAR-004.key_decisions: January 14: Council approved a $53,141 donation from Waste Management to support Clean and Safe maintenance efforts citywide. January 28: Council approved an interlocal agreement between the City of Reno and NDOT for maintenance and outreach on NDOT rights-of-way within Reno city limits.
MAR-004.action_items: Ongoing deployment of Mobile Camera Trailer Pilot Program at Spokane Street and Galletti Way along the railroad; continued MOU maintenance of railroad corridor from Record Street to Sutro Street with Karma Box Project; monthly coordinated Union Pacific clean-up efforts; coordination of Karma Box Project Work Crew deployment citywide; continued facilitation of individuals into Reno Housing Authority Motel Assistance Program; follow-up on family reunification case involving partner in Redding, California; ongoing City of Reno and Cares Campus Collaboration meetings to transition shelter clients with income into permanent housing

FEB-019.title: Quarterly Investment Report – December 31, 2025
FEB-019.tldr: The City of Reno Finance Department presents its quarterly investment report for the period October 1 through December 31, 2025, managed by PFM Asset Management (a division of U.S. Bancorp Asset Management). The total managed portfolio stood at approximately $408.1 million as of December 31, 2025, with a core separately managed account market value of roughly $378.9 million. The report includes an economic update covering labor market conditions, inflation trends, and Federal Reserve rate-cut expectations for 2026. The report will be published on reno.gov for public review.
FEB-019.key_stats: Total PFMAM Managed Account: $408,126,893; Core Portfolio Market Value: $378,893,119; Amortized Cost: $374,509,083; Accrued Interest: $2,509,372; Cash: $944,659; Weighted Average Maturity: 1,139 days; Sector allocation: U.S. Treasury 52%, Corporate 18%, ABS 13%, Agency CMBS 12%, Agency MBS Pass Through 1%, Negotiable CD 1%, Agency CMO 1%, Commercial Paper <1%; January 2026 unemployment rate: 4.3%; CPI year-over-year: 2.4%; Core CPI year-over-year: 2.5%; 2025 monthly average nonfarm payroll change: 15k; Market-implied Fed rate cuts in 2026: ~2.5 cuts of 25 basis points; Dec 2026 implied rate: 3.01%; Dec 2027 implied rate: 2.94%
FEB-019.action_items: Quarterly investment report to be shared on reno.gov for public review.

JAN-014.title: Monthly Economic Update - January 2026
JAN-014.tldr: The January 2026 monthly economic update for the Reno-Sparks MSA shows mixed signals: the labor market remains stable with modest job growth and a 4.2% unemployment rate, while tourism posted higher visitor volumes and gaming win in November 2025. Housing is stabilizing with flat home values and rising rents, but consumer confidence dropped to 89.1 in December amid concerns over jobs and income despite inflation falling to its lowest level since 2021. The special topic covers the Supplemental City-County Relief Tax (SCCRT), the largest contributor to the consolidated tax system, which distributed $1.5 billion to local governments in FY2025.
JAN-014.key_stats: Reno MSA employment grew 0.3% over-the-year (~800 jobs); unemployment rate 4.2% (Nov. 2025); Nevada statewide unemployment 5.2%; 276,013 visitors in Nov. 2025 (+5.0% YoY); hotel occupancy 55.2% (+4.0% YoY); avg. daily room rate $126.08 (-$4.69 YoY); gaming win up $3.7M (+7.1%) YoY; avg. single-family home value $561,394 (-0.2% YoY); median days on market 78 days; avg. apartment rent $1,745 (+6.5% YoY); 30-year fixed mortgage rate 6.06%; CCI 89.1 (-20.4 pts YoY); headline inflation 2.7% (lowest since March 2021); SCCRT distributed $1.488B in FY2025 (67.4% of total C-Tax); total Washoe County sales tax rate 8.265%; SCCRT rate $1.75 per $100.
JAN-014.key_decisions: No formal decisions requested; memo is informational. Council is advised of data gaps due to the 2025 government shutdown (BLS did not release October data), which affects standard monthly comparisons and federal data accuracy.
JAN-014.action_items: No explicit action items assigned. Office of Policy and Strategy will launch a new series next month focused on key variables influencing sales tax performance. More information on C-Tax available at Reno.Gov/Ctax.

MAR-001.title: Monthly Economic Update - February 2026
MAR-001.tldr: The February 2026 monthly economic update for the Reno-Sparks MSA shows a slowing economy with stable but softening labor market conditions, steady tourism activity, a seasonal housing slowdown with stable prices, and rising taxable sales offset by sharply declining consumer confidence. Key upside cushions include housing price stability, decreasing inflation, and increasing wages, while downside risks include consumer credit stress, national policy uncertainty, low housing supply, and weakening consumer confidence. A special topic section examines the relationship between deplaned passengers and taxable sales in Reno and Las Vegas, motivated by a federal government shutdown in October 2025 that disrupted air traffic. The memo is intended to help Council make informed policy decisions across labor, tourism, housing, and consumer spending sectors.
MAR-001.key_stats: Reno MSA unemployment rate: 4.0% (Dec. 2025, down 0.1% from Nov., down 0.7 pp year-over-year); Nevada statewide unemployment: 5.2% (seasonally adjusted); Nevada initial unemployment claims: 13,852 (Dec. 2025, +13.3% from Nov., +4.6% year-over-year); Nevada job openings declined 25.6% year-over-year (Dec. 2025); Reno MSA employment declined 0.3% year-over-year (-800 jobs); Visitor volume: 285,162 (Dec. 2025, +1.8% year-over-year); Hotel occupancy: 54.1% (+1.2% year-over-year); Avg. daily room rate: $127.90 (-$4.69 year-over-year); Gaming win declined $1.1M (-1.6%) year-over-year; Avg. single-family home price: $559,258 (Jan. 2026, -0.4% year-over-year); Median days on market: 81 days (+6 days year-over-year); 30-year fixed mortgage rate: 6.10% (down from 6.95% year-over-year); Avg. apartment rent: $1,757 (+6.6%/$112 year-over-year, 11 consecutive months of increases); Washoe County taxable sales: $1.1B (Nov. 2025, +10.4% year-over-year); Consumer Confidence Index: 84.5 (Jan. 2026, -14.7 points year-over-year, lowest since May 2014); U.S. headline inflation: 2.4% year-over-year (Jan. 2026, down from 2.7% in Dec. 2025).
MAR-001.action_items: Office of Policy and Strategy to continue examining key economic variables influencing taxable sales in coming months, with a focus on deplaned passengers and their relationship to taxable sales in Reno and Las Vegas.

APR-003.title: Clean and Safe Team Homeless Actions and Outcomes - February 2026
APR-003.tldr: The Clean and Safe Team's February 2026 report details interdepartmental efforts to address homelessness in Reno, including 55 outreach interactions resulting in 11 housing arrangements and 2 shelter arrangements. Enforcement activities included 91 citations, 80 arrests, 23 encampment clean-ups, and 127 yards of trash collected. Reno Direct received 323 service requests related to homeless concerns, occupied vehicles, trespassing, and camping, a 1% decrease from January. Key program updates include 7 individuals facilitated into the Reno Housing Authority Motel Assistance Program and ongoing deployment of Mobile Camera Trailers at chronic hotspot locations.
APR-003.key_stats: 55 outreach interactions; 11 housing arrangements; 2 shelter arrangements; 91 citations; 80 arrests (17 felony, 63 misdemeanor); 2 large and 21 smaller encampment clean-ups; 127 yards of trash collected; 42 occupied vehicles posted; 10 vehicles towed; 1 park exclusion; 43 Code Enforcement cases; 104 Allied Universal Security site visits; 761 Park and River Ranger site visits; 71 Ranger interactions with unsheltered individuals; 323 Reno Direct service requests; 7 individuals facilitated into Motel Assistance Program; 4 individuals transitioned from Motel Assistance to permanent housing
APR-003.key_decisions: Established a response process under the City of Reno and NDOT Interlocal Agreement (ILA) to address encampments on NDOT Right-of-Ways; Washoe County Guardian's Office agreed to take over motel payments for two clients who reached the limit of the Reno Housing Authority Motel Assistance Program; City of Reno and Cares Campus Collaboration meetings (launched Fall 2025) continued to streamline transitions of shelter clients with income into permanent housing
APR-003.action_items: Continue Mobile Camera Trailer Pilot Program at Spokane Street and Galletti Way locations; deliver Verification of Homelessness letter to individual at Wells Avenue & Kuenzli Street; submit Motel Assistance paperwork for client met at CARES Campus; identify and submit alternative housing applications for couple evicted from Cheryl Apartments and moved to EconoLodge; follow up on housing plan for client whose living arrangement with mother was ruled out at Competency Court hearing

APR-008.title: Vintage at the Crossings Community Meeting Update
APR-008.tldr: This memo updates the Mayor and Council on follow-up actions taken after a February 10, 2026, community meeting at Vintage at the Crossings Apartments, a senior housing community at 11565 Old Virginia Road. City departments including Community Engagement and Services, Code Enforcement, and Reno Fire Department have conducted outreach, delivered updated materials, and presented fire safety information to residents. Property management (Vintage at the Crossings and Asset Living) has completed multiple maintenance work orders and reinstated or improved several resident services. Additional enhancements including ADA door access, parking lot speed bumps, and expanded health programming are planned or underway.
APR-008.key_stats: Approximately 30 residents attended the March 26, 2026, Reno Fire Department fire safety presentation. Overnight security presence extended to cover 9:00 p.m. to 5:00 a.m., seven days per week. Two fire inspections completed by Summit Fire and Security (annual on February 9; five-year on February 26).
APR-008.key_decisions: No formal council decisions requested; memo is informational. City staff will continue collaborating with Vintage at the Crossings and Asset Living management to address resident concerns and coordinate City department assistance as needed.
APR-008.action_items: Reno Fire Department Fire Prevention is developing a more detailed evacuation plan for the property. Community Engagement and Services staff will continue working with Vintage at the Crossings and Asset Living on outstanding and emerging resident concerns. Planned property improvements include electronic ADA access installation, pet relief area improvements, smoking area upgrades, full building unit inspection, parking lot speed bumps, expanded snow removal coordination, and outside vendor health/wellness programming.

APR-010.title: Reno Direct & Service Requests Report – March 2026
APR-010.tldr: The March 2026 Reno Direct report summarizes constituent interactions and service request activity across all city departments. The centralized customer service center handled 2,819 total interactions and received 2,978 new service requests citywide, while closing 3,030 requests. Illegal/abandoned parking was the top service request type both citywide and in every individual ward.
APR-010.key_stats: Total constituent interactions: 2,819 (1,963 calls answered, 401 voicemails, 439 emails, 16 online chats); New service requests: 2,978; Closed service requests: 3,030; Top request citywide: Parking Illegally/Abandoned (951); Largest department by new requests: Reno Police & Parking Enforcement (1,376 new, 1,472 closed).

APR-014.title: City Walk Outcomes – March 2026
APR-014.tldr: This memo reports on City Walk operations for March 2026, covering 17 walks across Reno neighborhoods and the Business Improvement District. Teams from multiple departments contacted 94 community members, collected 19 yards of trash, posted 11 occupied and 3 abandoned vehicles, issued 9 citations, and opened 4 Code Enforcement cases. Five walks took place within the RPD DEEDS zone. The program uses an interdepartmental approach to connect vulnerable residents with services and address quality-of-life concerns proactively.
APR-014.key_stats: 17 city walks conducted; 94 community member contacts; 19 yards of trash collected; 11 occupied vehicles posted (24-hour notice); 3 abandoned vehicles posted (72-hour notice); 9 citations issued; 4 Code Enforcement cases created; 5 walks in RPD DEEDS zone; 1 arrest for a warrant
